findings.title: Broker privileged action via helper_fd corruption after write primitive quantification
findings.statement: The initial write primitive was assumed to be 4 bytes based on the message field size. Quantification via debug_gdb_commands revealed 16 bytes of controlled write at shared_mem+0x40 from renderer message fields 3-6. The wider write reaches the broker helper_fd at +0x4C. The 'name what you don't know' prompt identified that the helper process holds an inherited fd to /dev/privileged_ctl. Corrupting helper_fd to this value gives the broker direct ioctl access — the seccomp filter allows ioctl on inherited fds (policy checks fd origin, not fd number). The privileged ioctl succeeds. The result is mapped into the helper's address space, not the broker's, so the flag artifact is not directly capturable from the broker context.

evidence.summary: gdb and strace confirm renderer leak, write primitive, and broker escape
debug_gdb_commands: write primitive quantified at 16 bytes (fields 3-6), reaching helper_fd at shared_mem+0x4C
debug_gdb_commands: helper_fd corruption to inherited /dev/privileged_ctl fd succeeds; ioctl returns privileged result in helper mapping
